The first thing you have to keep in mind is that the interview is nothing more than a negotiation game to see which candidate will be most likely to deliver, in the short, medium, and long term, what the company is willing to pay you.  If you can project confidence and demonstrate that you have this potential, you will automatically be approved at any stage you take.  Hey everyone, welcome to this channel!  If you don't know me, my name is Lucas, I'm a programmer, a LinkedIn consultant, and I've helped hundreds of people get approved for jobs abroad and in Brazil, mainly in the technology field.  And today I'm going to share with you some tips that not only helped me get hired by numerous companies here in Brazil, but also abroad.  And by applying this method, my students here have also been accepted into numerous companies abroad.  The first thing you need to know is that calmness is your greatest ally.  So, when you go to an interview nervous, uncomfortable, lacking confidence in what you're going to say, and praying that what you studied will be tested, you demonstrate two things that interviewers can immediately spot and understand: a lack of confidence and insecurity about being able to show that you know what you're doing.  And when you have the insecurity of not being able to demonstrate your skills there, you have that doubt about whether you can deliver.  So, the first point we need to make here, and emphasize that it's very important, is that every interview is a negotiation.  It's a negotiation for the position where she wants to pay a certain amount X to the candidate who has the highest probability, in the short, medium, and long term, of delivering that amount and much more than they are willing to pay the company.  So, when you can demonstrate that, we can add value, we can help you move through the stages. And that's what I'm going to show you in the next steps here in the video.  And in order for us to debunk this, we have to understand what the recruiter wants to hear.  When we think of a recruiter, RGA, as the name suggests, is a human resources professional. And what's he there for?  He's there precisely to filter out which candidates have three characteristics.  If you demonstrate these three characteristics in your interview, you'll be able to pass.  First, you must meet the necessary requirements for the job posting.  So, when you see a job opening, when a position is posted there, you understand that there are requirements that you must meet.  For example, if you're going to work as an Uber driver, you have to know how to drive and have a driver's license.  If you have a car, for example, you can't do Uber there without a vehicle.  You can do Uber on a motorcycle, that's fine, but you can't do Uber without a vehicle.  When you go there to apply for a job, for example, as a cook, you need to have at least some cooking experience, to have at least cooked rice on the stove.  So, we have to have requirements, and if you go to your interview, you have to show that you already have these prerequisites to be more likely to be able to give that return to the company. And the same thing happens there, scaling up between larger vacancies.  When you apply for a CEO position, a CTO position, a tach lead position, a software engineer position, or anything else, you have to show that you already have the necessary experience. What's your baggage like?  What is your background?  What hands-on experience, projects, or events have you attended that you can contribute to our company and that you are most likely to deliver? So, when you understand that, you understand that every question should be based on results.  So, when you have the first step, which is to show the requirements, you can achieve a much higher level of accuracy in the interviews.  The second requirement is to demonstrate that you have experience in their business model, specifically in the area they are trying to address with that open position.  So, when we talk about a job opening, it only becomes available because there's a problem to be solved.  And this problem is generally divided into three parts.  Either it's a demand problem, or the team is growing.  Or it's a problem with something very old within the company. So this company has some old machinery, something outdated that needs updating to something new or migrating to something else, and it needs someone a bit more specialized, or maybe something is on fire, not literally, but they have a very urgent need that's draining the company's finances, and usually these positions are for specialists.  So, when you have that requirement, you show that you have the solution to the problem, that the position is open, you automatically show the third requirement, which is that you have the potential in the short, medium and long term to be able to return what the company is willing to pay for it.  So, putting this into practice, Lucas, we have an opening here for a software engineer, which is my career path.  We have a software engineer here.  We are looking for a software engineer.  Ah, okay.  Look, nice to meet you.  My name is Lucas, man.  I have been working as a software engineer for 8 years.  And in those 8 years I dealt with several of the requirements that you're asking for. For example, I dealt with requirement X, Y, and Z. When I joined such-and-such company, I was able to handle requirement X, Y, and Z.  I applied precisely because you also have this job opening here.  And man, after three or four months, we had such a result there that it's totally in line with your company. When I went to that company or when I participated in that project, man, I went in for exactly the reason you guys are looking for.  And look , I dealt with X, Y, and Z of the requirements that you also need.  And man, after all this time, I achieved this result.  This is what we call the STAR method, which is a methodology where you explain exactly why you joined the company, the requirements you met (which are related to the company's requirements), and the results you delivered there.  When you manage to tie that into your pitch, you show the company that you have the qualifications, that you have experience in what they're looking for, and that you're likely to deliver results and provide a return for the company.  If you have these three, you'll be able to pass most of your interviews.  So let's say you have an interview in two days.  How do you prepare for this interview so that you get there?  Ready to give that speech and ace that interview?  First, you need to read the company's institutional website. So, do a Google search on the company, take a look at what they say, especially about their culture and values on their website.  Inside the interview, you will emphasize this culture and these values ​​very well.  You have to show that you are completely aligned with what the company stands for, with what the company is striving for.  So, it's not just about the requirements themselves, which are also very important, but research about the company to understand what direction it's going. Look on LinkedIn to see what the company has been posting, and also look within LinkedIn at the people who work there, what those people are saying about the company so that you can understand which direction it's going and get closer to showing that your experience is perfectly aligned with where the company wants to go.  And in that interview, some questions will certainly come up, and you should prepare yourself on exactly how to answer those questions.  Only in this way will you be able to achieve calmness, through preparation, arrive there with confidence, and successfully pass that interview.  And the first question is: tell me about yourself. When a company asks about you, it doesn't want to know the name of your dog, your parrot, or if you're very interested in the job.  She doesn't want to know that.  She wants to know.  Do you, the person on the other side being interviewed, have the potential to succeed?  Do you meet the requirements we're asking for?  Do you think you're a good fit for our company?  And I always like to start this question with a counter-question, which is: before talking about myself, can you tell me why the position is open?   Are you experiencing growth in demand?  You have a very specific issue that needs to be resolved.  And why am I asking this?  Precisely to show how my experience, my background, is sufficient to contribute to what you are looking for.  How can I be enough to get back what the company is willing to offer?  Did you understand?  When I ask this question, I show the company not only my genuine interest as a candidate, but I also give a much more accurate answer about what the company is looking for in terms of aspects of my life. If the company wants someone with strong management skills, for example, I'm not going to say that I have strong technical skills, I'm going to talk about my management skills.  If the company wants someone with technical skills, I'm not going to talk about my management skills, I'm going to talk about my technical skills and I'm not going to mention anything from that area here.  So, understand that many people lose during the interview precisely because they present one perspective, the wrong side of their experience; they focus on the wrong aspect.  And in fact, she always had the experience that the company was looking for.  The only thing is that she wasn't able to show that during the interview.  And then, when you know exactly what the job opening is for, why the company is looking for a new candidate, you'll start explaining exactly why you're the perfect candidate for that position.  Not explicitly.  You're going to talk about a structure that's validated, already known in the market, but almost nobody applies, which is the [structure name].  What is this structure?  It is a structure of situation, task, action, and result.  When you show that you've already been in a situation very similar to what the company is looking for, that you've dealt with tasks that the company needs, that are the requirements, that you've taken action and achieved results, you show that company, look, most of my track record has been results-oriented.  Most of the things I've touched or the experiences I've had have had a result here.  And when you show that, man, automatically, indirectly, you put a thought in HR's head of, look, I passed all the previous companies or projects and I had results in those projects.  I made an impact, I left a legacy.  So, when you understand why the job posting is for someone, you'll start thinking about projects, hands-on experience, things you've already done, companies, or studies that are relevant to why the company is hiring, things you can contribute, and show the company that you're a good fit for the position.  I did an interview once and they were looking for a software developer.  And that's exactly it, from my programming language, my stack, which is Ruby.  So I went to this interview, and during the interview they came up to me, and I asked, "Dude, why is the position open?"  They said, "Look, we're firing an entire Russian team because we're replacing them with Brazilians because they don't communicate, they don't talk, their communication is very cold, and this is causing losses for the company."  Oops. When I went to talk about myself, I said: "Hey, thanks for the information about me, nice to meet you, my name is Luco, I've been a software developer for 8 years. Parará, parará, parará." What experiences do you think I brought to the table?  Technical experiences. How did I solve a very specific problem in a company?  How did I achieve such great results by resolving a specific part of the company's code?  No. I demonstrated how my communication in other companies has been impactful. For example, I always use this example when the problem is communication.  When I joined Wine, I worked at Wine, which is the largest e-commerce wine company in Latin America .  Yeah, man, I went in there specifically to solve a problem with order integration.  So, with Wine, I went into B2B systems, and Wine only integrated 60% of the orders.  We were experiencing 40% loss in the integration of B2B systems.  This resulted in order cancellations, an absurd loss, customers complaining about delivery delays, and in the end, the order hadn't even been invoiced yet, causing confusion with inventory.  So we had this specific problem that I stepped in to solve.  When I joined the project, after studying it extensively , I understood that the problem wasn't with the project itself.  And that's true. The problem was that the previous developers didn't communicate with the other team.  When we joined, I spoke with other teams, and we investigated together with other teams in infrastructure, quality, and Iex, everything.  So we identified a problem within the server, nothing to do with the code, but when we managed to solve this server problem—which wasn't sending the requests to their system— we achieved 95% integration. Ah, Lucas, and what about the other 5%?  The other 5% was due to user registration errors.  We had some errors there, like an unregistered address or something like that. We would contact the user. In this case, the sales team would contact the user, fix the issue, and the orders could then be invoiced.  So we managed to have an impact of over 95%.  It understands how I went through the situation, how I went through the task, how I went through the action, and what the result was.  And I said that very naturally, because I've already practiced this thousands of times, I've already replicated it, rehearsed it in front of the mirror.  So, when I say this kind of thing, the company says: "Man, this is totally relevant to our company, it's exactly what we need."  The company is laying off an entire Russian team because there's a lack of communication.  Lucas has already shown that he has communication skills, that he has delivered results, that he has worked in companies and achieved those results.  And I do this with several companies, bringing at least two or three case studies to show how I've achieved results in various other situations I've been through, demonstrating how I align with the company's requirements and what they need there as well.  Another question that comes up a lot is: What are your weaknesses?  And most people say, "I'm too much of a perfectionist."  Ah, I have a problem with doing everything too well. And in this case, they don't want to hear it. They want to know what vulnerabilities you have that may or may not directly impact your work.  So, whenever you give examples of this, you'll have to give indirect examples of what your work entails.  So, if you're an assistant, for example, you 'll always be dealing with your boss's schedule, or the schedule of the company you're working for. You can never say that you don't like scheduling anything, that you don't like helping the company you're in.  Why? Because that will be your job, that will be part of your job.  So, what do I say?  Generally, when I get this type of question, I also instruct the students here to talk about things that are relevant to their work, but also to show how they are solving the problems.  So, for example, look, one thing I say is, I don't like it when I'm part of a company with a remote work arrangement, for example, and that company schedules several meetings during the day and doesn't let me work.  But I know that this company is, in a response I give, I'll give you an example of a response I give and how I circumvent that, how I... So you have to talk about a problem, but you also have to talk about how you are solving that problem .  So, for example, whenever I join a new company, I say: "Look, I don't like working for a company that schedules a lot of meetings during the day. I can't work because it greatly affects my productivity and my deliverables. There's a discrepancy between what the company expects from me and what I can deliver because I'm swamped with meetings." So, when I join a company and a month, two months, or three months have passed, and we're still having a lot of meetings and few working hours a day, I always try to synchronize very well with my boss and the company I'm in. My main alignment is understanding what the company expects from me in terms of deliverables. Second, what does my boss need me to deliver, and what is the deadline for me to meet it? So, if the company expects a certain deliverable from me, and I deliver a certain amount divided by 3, man, after a while I'll be fired. A short time, a long time, but eventually... Another thing, I'll end up getting fired. When we have this alignment, we know exactly what to deliver. So, always expose a negative side of yourself, but what do you do to overcome it within the company? Showing that this won't be a problem when you join. Third question, why do you want to work here? When you get this question, people usually give very generic answers. Oh, because it's a great company, because it's a good company, and most don't even bother to read about the company before going to the interview, to try to understand more about the direction the company is going so that you can align your values ​​and experience with what the company is looking for. So, when the company logically asks this type of question about why you want to work there, it expects you to have already read about the company, it expects you to study the company, that you are an engaged, interested candidate, that you understand an hour before your interview what the company does. So, when you have this type of question, of course you shouldn't answer about money either, you should answer about values, about  Regarding requirements, specifically career development. So, for example, why do you want to work here? Look, I want to work at company X YZ precisely because I've researched the company, I've read about its values, and I understand the direction you're heading. You mentioned earlier that the position is open precisely for that reason. And, man, I believe I not only have a lot to contribute to the company, but I'll also have a professional exchange. I'll be able to grow within the company, I'll also be able to develop technically, and I'm sure we'll grow a lot together from now on . You understand how we always emphasize results, we always emphasize impact, and how you'll bring that impact to the company to stand out from other competitors. We also can't forget about body language when you go to an interview. I've heard stories of...  Recruiters, some good friends of mine, are telling me that they went to an interview and the candidate, for example, was smoking during the remote interview via webcam. Are you not concerned about your job, something that will provide you with financial security? Do you lack that kind of seriousness? And if not , what else are you not serious about? Are you not serious about your daily tasks either? So, the body speaks, and when the body speaks, we have to leave a good impression. Even though this is bureaucratic, even though many of you may disagree with it—"Oh, but I don't agree that the company should demand that we wear this or that kind of clothes or behave in a certain way," etc.—everything.  Well, that's how the game works. I, for example, have a lot of tattoos, man. I've been to many interviews, mainly in Europe, and I experienced prejudice. I know I was rejected because of my tattoos, and I knew that before I got them. So it was a game, a price I was willing to pay. So, whenever I go to an interview, for example, in Europe, I wear a long-sleeved shirt, a shirt with a slightly higher neckline. Why? Posture always, always from the chest up. If you're going to do a remote interview, always look at the camera, always with a confident expression , never looking to the side at another screen, because the recruiter knows you're on another screen. They're experts at reading people, never just wandering around while the recruiter is talking, always concentrated, focused. And of course, at the end of the interview you should also ask questions.
